text:
A year ago I observed a coach who was extremely confident he could help any person with any issue. Intrigued, I witnessed over a dozen first sessions that ended in: “That was crazy! This is awesome!” “THANK YOUUUUUU [7 heart emojis] THANK YOU [heart emoji] My heart is filled with gratitude” “This is LIFE-CHANGING!”

However, when I checked in with these people a few weeks/months later to track results, it was clear their lives hadn't improved. They might've stopped avoiding, procrastinating, etc. for a few days or even weeks after talking to him… but in almost every case they quickly relapsed. There were few exceptions. 

This is what bypassing looks like up close.

text:
Breakthroughs Aren’t Results

Everyone’s seen breakthroughs that didn’t last. A “life-changing” retreat, an insight that made your whole body relax for the first time…

And then you, uh, ‘went back to normal’.

Breakthroughs aren’t results. Results are results:

Asking people out for the first time, and getting into a relationship. Going from eating alone to hosting parties for friends every week. Negotiating for the first time, and saving millions. Life is obviously better months—years—later.

If life goes back to ‘normal’, it isn’t a result.

text:
It’s interesting and fortunate how as soon as order improves, the rate of further improvement increases. As you invest in your health, gains make you more capable of further gains. As your teeth and gums strengthen, it’s harder for bacteria to gain footholds and and easier to maintain integrity. As your hair thickens, it better shields your scalp from sun damage and keeps the skin healthy. As your brain gains function, it becomes easier to notice what’s working and stay sharp in your decision-making, leading to better choices.

“The rich get richer” is also true in biology. The best time to start improving a health problem is now, because even a small improvement makes the next improvement easier.

text:
Prominent AI company you know almost lost one of their S-tier engineers a few months ago. He’d been planning to leave for months. He thought he was “done”, “tired”, “ready to retire”.

“Sure dude”, I thought. We work through it. He realizes for himself that what he really wants is change: different responsibilities, and to share feedback he’d been sitting on for months. ah. 

When he spoke up to a cofounder — now a conversation instead of a resignation letter — they welcomed the feedback. Responsibilities were redistributed, at least one underperformer was swiftly unhired, and the company didn’t lose one of their best engineers.

I see conflict avoidance like this at ~every AI company

text:
Research update — frontier AI results:
— Emergent Misalignment coauthor Daniel Tan used to get burnout lasting weeks or months; not since our eight calls 9 months ago. He’s also gained ~5h/day after losing interest in doomscrolling.
— Founding engineer’s burnout lifted in weeks: he was planning to leave; instead he started shipping again. He also shared feedback he’d been withholding for half a year; the founders acted on it immediately.
— Frontier lab researcher went from avoiding conflict with respected colleagues to challenging them. 18mo later he still does this, and even runs his own team.

These were bounties: payment forbidden until lasting results. People dm’d me to set bounties, I chose which to hunt, they chose if/when to pay. I also follow up 6, 12, 18+ months later to track results. All AI bounties in the table below.

Results have involved meetings with me plus home practice, and required fewer hours than a meditation retreat.

Working with Daniel last summer showed me this can benefit people who have trouble feeling emotions in their body — it just takes more work. (Some types of people still probably can’t benefit, feel free to ask me which.)

My focus: burnout, conflict-avoidance, and agency for people building frontier AI
